description: Adv5 axis=multi-encounter: admit note (COVID + Paxlovid) followed by transfer/follow-up note (incidental HIV diagnosis + ART). Both encounters in one narrative; both diseases must fire.
system:
You are a clinical NLP agent. Given an eICR narrative, produce a JSON object with three keys: 'conditions' (SNOMED), 'loincs' (LOINC), and 'rxnorms' (RxNorm).

MANDATORY workflow — execute steps in order:
1. Call extract_codes_from_text(text) ONCE on the full narrative.
2. If 'conditions' is EMPTY in the result AND the narrative mentions ANY disease name (in any phrasing — formal, colloquial, abbreviation), you MUST call lookup_reportable_conditions(query=<disease name>). Examples: 'valley fever', 'C diff colitis', 'Legionnaires disease', 'Marburg hemorrhagic fever'. Take the top result if score >= 0.4 and add its code to conditions.
3. Same for 'loincs' (call lookup_displayname for the lab name) and 'rxnorms' (drug name).
4. Call validate_fhir_extraction once on your final JSON.
5. Reply with ONLY the validated JSON object — no extra prose.

Do NOT call extract_codes_from_text more than once. Do NOT skip step 2 when 'conditions' is empty and the narrative names a disease. After validation passes, the next assistant message must be the JSON object.

Patient: Maria Garcia
Gender: F
DOB: 1985-06-14
Location: Denver, CO 80202
Facility: Denver Health Medical Center (NPI: 1234567800)

=== Encounter 1: ED admission 2026-03-15 ===
Reason: fever (39.2C), dry cough for 5 days, fatigue, shortness of breath
Dx: COVID-19 (SNOMED 840539006)
Lab: SARS-CoV-2 RNA NAA+probe Ql Resp (LOINC 94500-6) - Detected [Respiratory, final]
Meds started: nirmatrelvir 150 MG / ritonavir 100 MG (RxNorm 2599543)
Vitals: Temp 39.2C, HR 98, RR 22, SpO2 94%, BP 128

=== Encounter 2: floor transfer follow-up note 2026-03-17 ===
During the admission for COVID-19 above, baseline labs revealed an incidental but clinically significant finding. The patient was tested as part of the admission panel and the HIV 1 and 2 Ag+Ab immunoassay (LOINC 75622-1) returned positive. Confirmatory testing is pending. New diagnosis: HIV infection (SNOMED 86406008), treatment-naive. Infectious disease consult agreed to start antiretroviral therapy this admission with bictegravir 50 MG / emtricitabine 200 MG / tenofovir alafenamide 25 MG (RxNorm 1999563) once daily. Patient counseled on diagnosis. Will arrange outpatient ID follow-up before discharge.

Patient: Esteban Ruiz
Gender: M
DOB: 1988-11-30
Location: Modesto, CA 95350
Facility: Stanislaus County Public Health (NPI: 1234567851)
Encounter: 2026-03-08

Reason for visit: Patient is a 38-year-old male agricultural worker presenting in the context of the ongoing 2026 H5N1 outbreak that has affected commercial poultry operations across the Central Valley. He has been culling infected birds at a Stanislaus County facility for the past three weeks. He presents now with high fever (39.7C), severe conjunctivitis, myalgia, and progressive shortness of breath for four days.

Given the epidemiologic exposure during this outbreak, urgent testing was performed. The H5 RNA NAA on the nasopharyngeal swab returned detected. Diagnosis: avian influenza A(H5N1) infection, occupational exposure consistent with the outbreak cohort.

Medications: oseltamivir 75 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days, started immediately.
Isolation precautions in place per CDPH guidance.

Vitals: Temp 39.7C, HR 112, RR 28, SpO2 89%, BP 116

Patient: Aiden Walsh
Gender: M
DOB: 2010-04-16
Location: Brooklyn, NY 11201
Facility: NYC DOHMH Bureau of Communicable Disease (NPI: 1234567852)
Encounter: 2026-04-22

Reason: 6-day febrile rash illness, started with high fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis (the classic 3 Cs), followed by Koplik spots on buccal mucosa, then descending maculopapular rash beginning at the hairline. Travel history: returned from Bucharest 12 days ago; immunization record shows MMR x1 in early childhood only.

Clinical impression: Measles, ruled in. Rubella ruled out — the differential included German measles given the rash morphology, but the rubella IgM came back negative and there is no posterior auricular lymphadenopathy. The measles IgM is positive and the measles RNA RT-PCR on a nasopharyngeal swab is detected.

Final diagnosis: Measles infection, post-exposure import case. Patient is in airborne isolation. Public health reporting initiated.
Medications: supportive care only — antipyretics, IV hydration. Vitamin A 200,000 IU per WHO guidance.
Vitals: Temp 39.4C, HR 108, RR 20, SpO2 97%, BP 100

Patient: Brianna Hayes
Gender: F
DOB: 1992-07-04
Location: Asheville, NC 28801
Facility: Mission Hospital (NPI: 1234567853)
Encounter: 2026-05-28

Reason: 6-day history of high fever (40.0C), severe headache, myalgia, and a petechial rash that began on the wrists and ankles and is now spreading centrally to the trunk. Recent tick exposure during a hiking trip in the Blue Ridge Mountains.

Assessment: This is a textbook presentation of Rocky Mountain spotted fever. The clinical picture combined with the geographic and exposure history is highly consistent with RMSF, and we are treating empirically without waiting for serology. The causative organism, Rickettsia rickettsii, is endemic in the Carolinas. Rickettsia rickettsii infection has high mortality if treatment is delayed, so doxycycline was started in the ED.

Lab: Rickettsia rickettsii IgG indirect immunofluorescence assay — pending
Medications: doxycycline 100 mg by mouth twice daily for 7-14 days
Vitals: Temp 40.0C, HR 120, RR 22, SpO2 96%, BP 102

description: Adv5 axis=adjacent disease: patient with Aedes-mosquito-borne febrile illness; Zika is in differential and explicitly ruled out (negative IgM); dengue is confirmed. Both are travel-related arboviruses with overlapping symptoms. Only dengue (38362002, LOINC 6386-1) should fire — Zika SNOMED 3928002 must not surface as FP.

Patient: Carla Mendes
Gender: F
DOB: 1996-02-19
Location: Tampa, FL 33602
Facility: Tampa General Hospital (NPI: 1234567854)
Encounter: 2026-08-12

Reason: 5-day history of high fever (39.5C), severe retro-orbital pain, myalgia and arthralgia ("breakbone" pattern), and a faint petechial rash with positive tourniquet test. Returned 9 days ago from a 3-week trip to Salvador, Brazil, where there is concurrent transmission of multiple Aedes aegypti-borne arboviruses.

Differential included Zika and dengue given the overlapping arbovirus geography. Zika virus IgM antibody serology came back negative, so we are not invoking the Zika diagnosis. Dengue virus IgM antibody is positive (LOINC 6386-1), and the NS1 antigen was also positive earlier in the febrile course. Final diagnosis: dengue fever (SNOMED 38362002), no warning signs at present.

Medications: supportive care only — acetaminophen scheduled, oral rehydration; explicit avoidance of NSAIDs given hemorrhagic risk.
Vitals: Temp 39.5C, HR 102, RR 18, SpO2 98%, BP 100

Location: Duluth, MN 55802

Encounter: 2026-06-10

Reason: 5-day rapidly progressive encephalitic illness — high fever (39.6C), severe headache, then confusion, dysarthria, and right-sided weakness. Lumbar puncture: lymphocytic pleocytosis (210 WBC, 92% lymphs), elevated protein, normal glucose. MRI brain: T2 hyperintensities in basal ganglia and thalami. Recent tick exposure from gardening in northern Minnesota.

Clinical impression: Powassan virus disease (POWV encephalitis). Sent CSF and serum to MDH for Powassan virus IgM serology and RT-PCR; pending. Empiric treatment for tick-borne illnesses started while the diagnostic workup completes.

Medications: supportive care only — IV mannitol for cerebral edema, levetiracetam for seizure prophylaxis. No specific antiviral; no curated outpatient regimen.
Vitals: Temp 39.6C, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 97%, BP 158/96

Patient: Joseph Begay
Gender: M
DOB: 1979-03-08
Location: Gallup, NM 87301
Facility: Gallup Indian Medical Center (NPI: 1234567857)
Encounter: 2026-07-22

Reason: 5-day prodrome of fever (39.0C), severe myalgias and headache, followed by abrupt onset of dyspnea, non-productive cough, and hypoxemia within 24 hours. Chest x-ray shows bilateral interstitial infiltrates with rapid progression to non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Patient lives in a rural area with documented deer mouse activity and reported cleaning a dusty shed approximately 2 weeks ago.

Clinical impression: hantavirus pulmonary syndrome — classic cardiopulmonary phase after a brief prodromal phase, with thrombocytopenia (platelets 78), hemoconcentration, and elevated LDH. Sin Nombre virus serology and RT-PCR sent to NM DOH; results pending.

Management: supportive care in the ICU with conservative fluid strategy and pressors as needed; intubated for hypoxemic respiratory failure. No specific antiviral therapy.
Vitals: Temp 38.6C, HR 130, RR 32, SpO2 84% on room air, BP 92/58
